title: Elements, Atoms, and Atomic Particles
narrationScript: Everything radiography does, generating X rays, emitting gamma rays, absorbing radiation in steel, darkening film grains, happens at the scale of the atom. So before we can talk seriously about sources and exposures, we need to meet the cast of characters. Matter, elements, atoms, molecules, compounds, and the three particles that build them all. Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space. The steel in a pressure vessel, the air in this room, the film in your cassette. All matter is built from elements, the basic chemical building blocks that cannot be broken into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means. About ninety elements occur in nature, from hydrogen, the lightest, up through uranium, and more than one hundred fifteen are known once we count the man made ones. Iron, lead, cobalt, iridium. Each one is an element with its own personality. The atom is the smallest piece of an element that still behaves like that element. Atoms are unimaginably small, around a ten billionth of a meter across. A single glass of water contains more atoms than there are glasses of water in all the oceans of the world. Cut an iron atom into smaller pieces and you no longer have iron at all, just loose particles. Atoms rarely work alone. A molecule is two or more atoms bonded together, and it is the smallest natural unit of many substances. When the bonded atoms are the same element, like the oxygen molecule with its two oxygen atoms, we have a molecule of an element. When atoms of different elements bond, we get a compound, a completely new substance with its own properties. Water is the classic example. Two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. Hydrogen burns, oxygen feeds fire, and yet water puts fire out. A compound is not a blend. Salt water is a mixture, and you can separate it again by evaporation. Water itself can only be taken apart by breaking chemical bonds. Why does a radiographer care about any of this? Because what a part absorbs depends on what it is made of. Density and composition, which elements are present and how tightly they are packed, decide how much radiation gets through to your film. Now go one level deeper. Every atom is built from just three particles. Protons, neutrons, and electrons. The proton lives in the nucleus and carries one unit of positive charge. Its mass is about one atomic mass unit. The number of protons is the atom's identity card. Twenty six protons is iron, every time, no exceptions. The neutron also lives in the nucleus and has almost exactly the same mass as the proton, but it carries no charge at all. It is nuclear ballast and nuclear glue, helping hold the protons together. And as we will see in the next lesson, the neutron is the particle we fire into cobalt and iridium inside a reactor to manufacture radiography sources. The electron is the lightweight of the family. It carries one unit of negative charge, exactly equal and opposite to the proton's charge, but only about one two thousandth of the proton's mass. The precise ratio is one to one thousand eight hundred thirty six. Electrons move around the nucleus in defined shells, and they are the social side of the atom. Chemical bonding, electrical current, and most of the radiation absorption we exploit in radiography are electron business. In a neutral atom, the count of electrons equals the count of protons, so the charges cancel exactly. Strip an electron away and the balance breaks. The atom becomes a charged particle called an ion. That process, ionization, is precisely what X rays and gamma rays do to matter. It is how radiation exposes film, how your survey meter detects a field, and how radiation damages living tissue. Hold on to three simple facts from this lesson. Proton, positive charge, mass of one. Neutron, no charge, mass of one. Electron, negative charge, almost no mass. The entire periodic table, every weld you will ever shoot, and every source you will ever crank out is just these three particles in different arrangements.
imagePrompt: An instructor in a hi-vis vest points to a large wall chart of atomic structure while radiography trainees in work shirts take notes at steel benches in an industrial training classroom.

sections.heading: Matter and Elements
sections.body:
**Matter** is anything that has mass and occupies space - the steel of a pressure vessel, the air around it, the film in your cassette. All matter is assembled from **elements**: fundamental substances that cannot be broken into anything simpler by ordinary chemical means. About 90 elements occur in nature, from hydrogen (the lightest) through uranium, and over 115 are known including the man-made ones. Each element has a symbol (Fe for iron, Pb for lead, Co for cobalt, Ir for iridium) and a fixed set of properties.

Radiography keeps coming back to a handful of elements: **iron** in the steel we examine, **tungsten** in X-ray tube targets, **lead** in screens and shielding, **iridium**, **cobalt**, and **selenium** in gamma sources, and **silver** in film emulsion. How strongly a material absorbs radiation depends on which elements it contains and how densely they are packed - which is why a tungsten inclusion, lead shielding, and a steel weld each interact with the beam so differently.

sections.heading: Atoms: The Smallest Piece That Is Still the Element
sections.body:
An **atom** is the smallest particle of an element that retains the element's chemical properties. Atoms are around 10⁻¹⁰ m across - a hundred-millionth of a centimeter - so small that a single glass of water holds more atoms than the oceans hold glassfuls. Divide an iron atom further and you no longer have iron; you have a pile of subatomic particles that belong to no element at all.

That boundary matters for this course. **Chemistry** - rusting, burning, developing film - happens between whole atoms trading or sharing their outer electrons. The **nuclear** events that drive radiography - radioactive decay, gamma emission - happen inside the atom, in its nucleus, and no chemical process can start them, stop them, or speed them up. Keeping the chemical world and the nuclear world separate in your head is the first step toward understanding why a gamma source decays on its own schedule no matter what anyone does to it.

sections.heading: Molecules and Compounds
sections.body:
Atoms rarely stand alone. A **molecule** is two or more atoms chemically bonded together - the smallest naturally occurring unit of many substances.

- When the atoms are the **same element**, the result is a molecule of an element: oxygen gas (O₂) is two oxygen atoms bonded together. Still just oxygen.
- When atoms of **different elements** bond, the result is a **compound** - a genuinely new substance with its own properties. Water (H₂O) is the textbook case: hydrogen burns and oxygen feeds combustion, yet the compound extinguishes fire.

A compound is not a mixture. Salt dissolved in water is a **mixture** - each ingredient keeps its identity and evaporation separates them again. The water itself is a compound and can only be taken apart by breaking chemical bonds. Film emulsion is built on this distinction: the active ingredient is the compound **silver bromide**, whose behavior under radiation is nothing like metallic silver or liquid bromine alone.

For the radiographer, composition is half of absorption: a part's elements, their proportions, and its **density** decide how much of the beam reaches the film.

sections.caption: Element, molecule of an element, and compound - same building blocks, different combinations, different substances.

sections.heading: The Three Building Blocks
sections.body:
Every atom of every element is built from just three particles:

- The **proton** lives in the nucleus and carries one unit of **positive** charge (+1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ coulomb, the elementary charge). Its mass is about 1 atomic mass unit (u). The proton count is the atom's identity: 26 protons is iron, always.
- The **neutron** shares the nucleus, has nearly the same mass as the proton (about 1 u), and carries **no charge**. Neutrons add mass and help bind the nucleus together against the mutual repulsion of its positive protons - and a neutron fired into a nucleus is how reactors manufacture radiography sources.
- The **electron** orbits the nucleus in defined shells. It carries one unit of **negative** charge - exactly equal and opposite to the proton's - but only about **1/1836** of the proton's mass.

In a neutral atom, electrons equal protons and the charges cancel. Remove an electron and the atom becomes a positively charged **ion**; that event, **ionization**, is exactly what X-rays and gamma rays do to matter - it exposes film, fires survey meters, and damages tissue.

sections.caption: Properties of the three atomic particles.
rows: Changes the isotope; activation makes gamma sources
Produces X-rays; does most radiation absorption; ionization

sections.caption: A carbon-12 atom: six protons and six neutrons packed in the nucleus, with two electrons in the K shell and four in the L shell.

sections.title: Three particles run the whole method
sections.body: **Electrons** boiled off a filament and slammed into a target generate X-rays, and the electrons of the test part do most of the absorbing that forms the image. **Neutrons** captured in a reactor turn ordinary cobalt and iridium into gamma emitters. **Protons** decide what element - and therefore what absorber - you are dealing with. Learn the three particles now and every later lesson gets easier.

interactive.title: Proton, Neutron, or Electron?
interactive.intro: Sort each property or job onto the particle that owns it.

items.label: Carries one unit of positive charge
items.why: Protons are the positively charged particles of the nucleus.

items.label: Carries no electrical charge at all
items.why: The neutron is electrically neutral - it is in the name.

items.label: Carries one unit of negative charge
items.why: The electron's negative charge exactly balances a proton's positive charge.

items.label: Mass of only about 1/1836 of an atomic mass unit
items.why: Electrons are the featherweights; protons and neutrons each weigh about a full mass unit.

items.label: Its count alone decides which element an atom is
items.why: Atomic number = proton count = the element's identity.

items.label: Captured by iridium-191 in a reactor to create iridium-192
items.why: Neutron activation - adding a neutron to a stable nucleus - is how radiography sources are manufactured.

items.label: Occupies shells surrounding the nucleus
items.why: Electrons orbit in defined energy shells; protons and neutrons stay in the nucleus.

items.label: Adds about one mass unit to a nucleus without changing the element
items.why: Extra neutrons change the isotope and its mass, but the element is fixed by protons.

items.label: Equal in number to the electrons of a neutral atom
items.why: Neutral atoms balance protons against electrons so net charge is zero.

checkQuestions.question: Which of the following is a compound?
checkQuestions.explanation: Water is atoms of different elements chemically bonded into a new substance. O₂ is a molecule of one element, iron is an element, and salt water is a mixture that can be separated by evaporation.

checkQuestions.question: The smallest particle of an element that still retains the element's chemical properties is:
checkQuestions.explanation: That is the definition of the atom. Divide it further and you have subatomic particles that belong to no element.

checkQuestions.question: Which particle description is correct?
options: Proton: negative charge, mass about 1 u
Neutron: no charge, mass about 1 u
Electron: positive charge, mass about 1 u
checkQuestions.explanation: The neutron is electrically neutral with a mass close to one atomic mass unit - almost identical to the proton's mass.

checkQuestions.question: Compared with a proton, an electron has:
options: Equal and opposite charge, and about 1/1836 of the mass
Equal charge of the same sign, and equal mass
Half the charge, and 1/2 the mass
Opposite charge, and exactly the same mass
checkQuestions.explanation: The electron's charge is exactly equal in magnitude and opposite in sign to the proton's, but its mass is only about 1/1836 of the proton's.

checkQuestions.question: A neutral atom loses one orbital electron. The result is:
options: A positively charged ion - the atom has been ionized
checkQuestions.explanation: Removing a negative electron leaves the atom with one excess positive charge: a positive ion. Ionization is the mechanism by which radiography's radiation exposes film, triggers detectors, and damages tissue.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 2.1 (elements and atoms)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 2.2 (molecules and compounds)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 2.3 (atomic particles - protons, electrons, neutrons)
Elementary charge ≈ 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C; electron-to-proton mass ratio ≈ 1:1836 (reference values)
